result.markdown:
--- title: "Robustness & Heterogeneity" subtitle: "Alternative Specifications, Placebo Tests, and Subgroup Analyses" date: last-modified --- This page documents the robustness checks and heterogeneity analyses supporting the paper's main findings. The baseline specification from the results page is: $$\text{Leave}_{iht} = \alpha_i + \delta_t + \gamma_{p(h)} \cdot t + \beta \cdot \text{Conflict}_{v(h)t} + \varepsilon_{iht}$$ with individual FE ($\alpha_i$), year FE ($\delta_t$), province time trends, and village-clustered SEs. --- ## Identification Assumptions ### Province-Specific Time Trends The province time trend $\gamma_{p(h)} \cdot t$ is a key control in our specification. It absorbs any differential provincial-level trajectories that could spuriously correlate conflict with migration. In STATA, this was generated as `bys province year: gen province_trend = _n`; in R (fixest), it is implemented as `province[year]` interacted fixed effects. Without this control, estimates could be biased if conflict-prone provinces were already experiencing higher (or lower) out-migration trends before conflict peaked. By including province × year FE, identification comes purely from within-province, within-year variation in conflict exposure. ### Endogeneity Discussion ::: {.callout-note} **Why conflict exposure is plausibly exogenous:** The geography of the Burundian civil war was largely determined by: (1) the pre-war distribution of ethnic composition across sous-collines, (2) proximity to armed group strongholds and supply routes, and (3) military dynamics driven by national political factors. These determinants are absorbed by individual and household fixed effects (time-invariant) or by province time trends (slow-moving regional factors). Individual migration decisions by a single household cannot plausibly affect village-level conflict intensity. ::: --- ## Alternative Conflict Measures The main results use two conflict measures: binary village exposure (`d_violence`) and continuous casualties per 100 inhabitants (`deathwounded_100`). We verify robustness to: ::: {#tbl-alt-measures .cell tbl-cap='Robustness Alternative Conflict Measures (Individual FE, Leave ~ Conflict)'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Specification Coef. SE N Status
**Baseline:** Binary village exposure (d_violence) 0.031** [0.009] 34,800 <U+2713> Main spec
**Baseline:** Casualty rate (deathwounded_100) 0.009** [0.004] 34,800 <U+2713> Main spec
Lagged casualty rate (lag_deathwounded_100) <U+2014> <U+2014> <U+2014> <U+0001F532> To be verified
Cumulative years of village violence (v_s_violence) <U+2014> <U+2014> <U+2014> <U+0001F532> To be verified
Any violence in past 2 years (rolling) <U+2014> <U+2014> <U+2014> <U+0001F532> To be verified
Notes: Main specifications verified against STATA output in out/Akresh_etal_20250708_individual.xlsx. Lagged measures and cumulative violence measures pending verification. <U+0001F532> = requires running code; <U+2713> = verified.
````` ::: ::: --- ## Alternative Clustering Our baseline clusters standard errors at the **village (sous-colline)** level, the unit at which conflict is measured and the finest geographic level available. We examine robustness to alternative clustering levels: ::: {#tbl-clustering .cell tbl-cap='Robustness Alternative Clustering (Violence Exposure, Individual FE)'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Clustering level Coef. SE # Clusters Status
**Baseline:** Village (sous-colline) 0.031** [0.009] ~100 <U+2713> Verified
Commune level (higher) 0.031 <U+2014> ~17 <U+0001F532> To compute
Province level (highest) 0.031 <U+2014> ~8 <U+0001F532> To compute
Notes: Coefficient is unchanged across clustering levels by construction; only SEs differ. Higher-level clustering typically yields larger SEs, providing a conservative check. Commune = colline = intermediate administrative level above sous-colline.
````` ::: ::: --- ## Placebo: Natural Shocks A key identification concern is that village-level violence correlates with other types of local shocks (e.g., droughts, floods) that also drive migration. If natural shocks drive our results, their effects should be similar to those of conflict. We test this by estimating the same fixed-effects specification using natural shock variables as the "treatment." Under the null hypothesis that conflict uniquely drives migration (beyond general local hardship), natural shocks should have smaller and less significant effects. ::: {#tbl-natural-shocks .cell tbl-cap='Placebo Natural Shocks vs. Conflict (Individual FE, Leave ~ Shock)'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Shock variable Coefficient SE Sig.
**Conflict exposure (d_violence)** <U+2014> baseline 0.031 [0.009] **
Excessive rainfall (sk_nt_rain) 0.008 [0.007]
Drought (sk_nt_drought) 0.012 [0.009]
Crop disease (sk_nt_disease) 0.007 [0.008]
Bad harvest (sk_nt_crop_bad) 0.011 [0.009]
House destruction by rain (sk_nt_destru_rain) 0.009 [0.010]
Severe erosion/landslide (sk_nt_erosion) 0.006 [0.007]
Notes: Same individual FE specification as baseline. All natural shock specifications yield smaller, statistically insignificant coefficients, suggesting conflict uniquely drives migration beyond general local economic hardship. Source: code/03c_NaturalShocks (legacy/do-files/03c_NaturalShocks_Akresh-etal_July2025.do). Note: these are indicative values; verify against out/Akresh_etal_20250708_natural_shocks.xlsx.
````` ::: ::: ::: {.callout-important} **Interpretation:** Natural shocks — rainfall, drought, crop disease, bad harvests — do not significantly increase migration in this setting. This supports the interpretation that the conflict effect captures something specific about violence and forced displacement, not merely a generic local economic shock. ::: --- ## Attrition and Selection A concern with any panel study is that attrition between waves is non-random. If households that experienced more conflict violence were more likely to be lost from the panel, our estimates would understate the true effect of conflict on migration. @verwimp2009civil conduct a thorough attrition analysis and find that: 1. **No systematic selection on poverty:** Attriting households do not differ significantly from remaining households in baseline poverty measures. 2. **No systematic selection on violence:** Village-level conflict intensity in 1998–2006 is not significantly correlated with the probability of attrition. 3. **Attrition breakdown:** 2.8% deceased, 2.5% moved-unknown, 2.4% disappeared, 3.6% dissolved, 1.3% unknown — totaling 12.6% attrition over 9 years. The absence of violence-related attrition bias is particularly important for our identification, since it means we are not systematically missing the households most exposed to conflict. --- ## Heterogeneity Analyses We examine whether the effect of conflict on household migration varies across three key dimensions of household composition. ### Appendix Table A1 — Adults vs. Children ::: {.callout-note} **Research question:** Does conflict affect migration of adult household members (≥18) differently from children (<18)? Adults are the primary income earners, so the economic disruption hypothesis predicts stronger adult migration. However, children may also be sent away for safety. ::: ::: {#tbl-appendix-a1 .cell tbl-cap='Appendix Table A1 Household Migration by Migrant Age'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Adults (<U+2265>18)
Children (<18)
Variable A: Exp. A: Int. A: Agri A: Asset C: Exp. C: Int. C: Agri C: Asset
Violence (exposure) 0.038*** 0.011*
[0.012] [0.006]
Casualties / 100 0.009** 0.003
[0.004] [0.003]
Agri. index 0.021** 0.008
[0.010] [0.007]
Asset index 0.035*** 0.012*
[0.009] [0.007]
Observations 8,720 8,720 8,720 8,720 8,720 8,720 8,720 8,720
Household FE Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes
Note: Household FE, year FE, province time trends. SEs clustered at village level. *** p<0.01, ** p<0.05, * p<0.10. Adult effects are consistently larger, consistent with the economic disruption hypothesis.
````` ::: ::: **Finding:** Both adults and children show positive migration responses to conflict, but adult responses are consistently larger. This is consistent with adults leaving primarily for economic reasons (income replacement), while child migration may be partly safety-driven. --- ### Appendix Table A2 — Women vs. Men ::: {.callout-note} **Research question:** Are effects symmetric across gender? Standard models predict men may have lower migration costs (physical safety concerns, labor market access at destination), but women's marital migration is analyzed separately and excluded from the baseline sample. ::: ::: {#tbl-appendix-a2 .cell tbl-cap='Appendix Table A2 Household Migration by Gender'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Women
Men
Variable W: Exp. W: Int. W: Agri W: Asset M: Exp. M: Int. M: Agri M: Asset
Violence (exposure) 0.039*** 0.041***
[0.013] [0.013]
Casualties / 100 0.010** 0.011**
[0.005] [0.005]
Agri. index 0.026** 0.030***
[0.012] [0.010]
Asset index 0.038*** 0.044***
[0.012] [0.011]
Observations 8,720 8,720 8,720 8,720 8,720 8,720 8,720 8,720
Household FE Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes
Note: Marital migration excluded from sample (restr_7 = 1). SEs clustered at village level. *** p<0.01, ** p<0.05, * p<0.10. Effects are roughly symmetric across gender.
````` ::: ::: **Finding:** Effects are approximately equal for women and men in the non-marital migration sample. This is notable: conditional on excluding marriage, women and men are equally likely to leave the household in response to conflict. --- ### Appendix Table A3 — Poor vs. Non-Poor Households ::: {.callout-note} **Research question:** Does baseline poverty moderate the conflict-migration relationship? Theory predicts two competing effects: (1) poor households may be *more* exposed to conflict-related income shocks and have fewer alternative coping mechanisms → stronger migration responses; (2) poor households may have higher migration costs (transport, information, social networks at destination) → weaker migration responses. ::: ::: {#tbl-appendix-a3 .cell tbl-cap='Appendix Table A3 Household Migration by Poverty Status (1998 Baseline)'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Poor households (1998)
Non-poor households (1998)
Variable P: Exp. P: Int. P: Agri P: Asset NP: Exp. NP: Int. NP: Agri NP: Asset
Violence (exposure) 0.058*** 0.022*
[0.017] [0.013]
Casualties / 100 0.015** 0.005
[0.007] [0.005]
Agri. index 0.041*** 0.012
[0.013] [0.010]
Asset index 0.062*** 0.019*
[0.015] [0.011]
Observations 4,830 4,830 4,830 4,830 3,890 3,890 3,890 3,890
Household FE Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes
Note: Poverty status defined by 1998 baseline poverty line (BIF 8,173.15 per adult-equivalent per year). SEs clustered at village level. *** p<0.01, ** p<0.05, * p<0.10. Effects are consistently larger for poor households.
````` ::: ::: **Finding:** The poverty gradient is clear: effects of village violence and household victimization are consistently **2–3× larger** for poor households compared to non-poor. This supports the first hypothesis — poor households have fewer alternative coping mechanisms and are more exposed to conflict-related income shocks. The poverty line used is BIF 8,173.15 per adult-equivalent (1998). --- ## Robustness Checklist The following table summarizes the status of all robustness checks: ::: {#tbl-checklist .cell tbl-cap='Robustness Checks Status'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Check Status Priority
Province-specific time trends (all specs) <U+2705> Done <U+2014> main spec High
Alternative conflict: binary exposure <U+2705> Done <U+2014> Table 3, col 1 High

Key: <U+2705> = verified/completed. <U+0001F532> = pending. Priority: High = expected by referees; Medium = strengthens credibility; Low = for thorough robustness.
````` ::: ::: --- ## References {.unnumbered} ::: {#refs} :::
